data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Climate-induced agricultural failure triggered systemic collapse in interdependent palace economies due to lack of substitution mechanisms", "mechanism": "Prolonged drought and cooling (ca. 1200 BCE) reduced grain yields in Mediterranean rain-fed agriculture; palace centers with centralized redistribution lacked flexible market mechanisms to source alternative supplies, triggering cascading defaults in tribute-based trade networks, military wage obligations, and craft specialization, while Egypt's Nile-based irrigation (flood-independent of rainfall) and pre-existing grain surplus buffers maintained subsistence core despite territorial losses", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "Egyptian persistence as diminished survivor"], "excludes": ["Sea Peoples as primary cause", "purely political/military explanations without agrarian base", "Minoan collapse (earlier)", "Neolithic/pre-palace societies"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Nile flood levels show synchronous decline with palaeoclimate deterioration ca. 1200-1150 BCE", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian documentary record indicates palace economy with centralized redistribution comparable to Mycenaean wanax system rather than temple/private estate heterogeneity", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Anatolian pollen cores show no aridification signal in Hittite core territory during this period", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Ugarit archives indicate functioning private maritime trade networks independent of palace redistribution at collapse", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Emphasizes structural brittleness: palace systems as efficiency-optimized but fragility-prone, versus Egyptian 'inefficient' resilience through redundant institutional forms. Draws on network theory (percolation thresholds) and comparative institutional economics."}
